examples: MONEY LENT — the rates and terms in declining type
NOTICE — the rules of the station, what is permitted and what is not
POSITIONS AVAILABLE — the list, the requirements, the application process

authority behaviour.default: stamping forms — the stamp as power, its sound the institution's heartbeat
examples: assessing objects across the counter — the power to determine value
taking statements — writing the official version of events
reading applications — the power of selection

supplicant behaviour.default: waiting — the specific physical posture of those who need something from an institution
examples: presenting objects — the exposure of the desperate
explaining — the narrative the institution requires before it will act
waiting for the decision — the body in the posture of hope

institutional procedure.default: the queue advancing one person at a time — the stamp, the form, the next
examples: the assessment and the offer — the ritual of the pawnshop
the charge, the caution, the cell or the release — the police station's binary
the interview, the decision, the hire or the rejection — the employment process

institutional moment.default: a form rejected — the clerk's stamp on DENIED, the person's face, the queue continuing regardless
examples: an object refused — not enough value, the person's last resource insufficient
a charge dismissed — the machinery of justice briefly producing justice
a hire — the moment of relief in the face, the gate opening

institution ambient theme.default: bureaucratic march — repetitive, slightly grinding, the music of systems
examples: transaction theme — minor key commerce, the music of need meeting reluctant supply
authority theme — pompous and slow, the music of power at rest
hope theme — major key aspiration in a minor key institution

lens.default: 35mm equivalent — institution and supplicant in the same frame
examples: 28mm equivalent wide
50mm equivalent
24mm equivalent

tool_notes: Sora handles the complex period institutional environment. The architecture of power — high counters, glass barriers, elevated desks — is the social argument made in space. Add '1920s public institution, silent film world, orthochromatic, [Chaplin-era public institution], social power in architecture, Charlie Chaplin era, 4:3'.

Chaplin-era leisure environment.default: seaside resort — the working class day trip
examples: the park on a Sunday — democratic leisure
the dance hall — aspiration and its complications
the boxing arena — the working class spectacle

leisure setting.default: beach promenade and the sea behind — sun hats and swimming costumes
examples: public park — bandstand, paths, grass, benches
dance hall — wooden floor, small orchestra, paper decorations
boxing arena — ring, crowd, corner men, gamblers at the edges

class-specific leisure activity.default: working class families in the sea — paddling, building, running — joy at full volume
examples: Sunday park promenading — the public performance of respectability
dancing — the working class ambition of grace
watching the fight — the crowd as character

period leisure population.default: working class families — flat caps and bathing suits, children at the edge of the sea
examples: mixed classes in the park — the democratic Sunday
young working people — this is their ballroom, their stage
fight crowd — men in suits, women less present, the boxing crowd's specific demographic

leisure costume.default: period bathing costumes — modest by today's standards, brave for theirs
examples: Sunday best — the park demands the good coat
dance hall clothes — saved for and worn with intention
fight crowd clothes — working clothes elevated by the occasion

leisure architecture or setting detail.default: the promenade — the pier in the background, the beach below, the sea beyond
examples: the bandstand — a small orchestra visible, paths radiating from it
the dance floor — the orchestra at one end, couples across the space
the arena — the ring elevated, the crowd banked around it

leisure world event.default: a wave arriving larger than expected — the crowd's response
examples: the band striking up — the park's response
a couple who cannot agree on who leads — dancing anyway
a dramatic moment in the fight — the crowd as one body

Chaplin character potential.default: the Tramp at the beach's edge — watching more than participating, enjoying the sea from a respectful distance
examples: the outsider in the park — no Sunday best, full Sunday dignity
the worst dancer in the hall — making up in enthusiasm what he lacks in skill
the man with no bet — the only one watching the fight itself

leisure music style.default: seaside band — brass and percussion, the sound of the English resort
examples: park band — light orchestral, respectable and pleasant
dance band — syncopated, the period popular music
fight night — crowd noise musically represented, tension and release

lens.default: 35mm equivalent wide — the leisure world in its full social breadth
examples: 28mm equivalent
50mm equivalent
24mm equivalent — the full environment

tool_notes: Sora handles the complex period leisure environment. The Chaplin character potential — the figure who doesn't quite belong — is always available as a narrative seed. Add '1920s leisure world, silent film, orthochromatic, [Chaplin-era leisure environment], working class joy, Charlie Chaplin era, 4:3'. Joy and aspiration are the registers — not misery.

image_prompt: A [1920s transitional world moment] — orthochromatic silent film rendering, the frame containing [old world element] and [new world element] simultaneously: the world in the act of becoming something it has not yet finished becoming, [period population navigating change] visible in the frame going about their day in a world that is changing around them without their full awareness, [transition architecture or object] the frame's central visual argument — the old and new sharing the same street, the same moment, cinematic silent film transition world still, 4:3
video_prompt: Camera locked — the [1920s transitional world moment] playing out in continuous time, [old world element] and [new world element] operating in the same frame simultaneously: [old world activity] and [new world activity] as parallel realities, [period population navigating change] moving between them with the specific unawareness of people inside a historical transition, [transition event] occurring that briefly makes the [1920s transitional world moment] visible to someone in the frame — one person registering it — then the continuous movement of the world resuming, the transition continuing unannounced.
sfx_prompt: Silent film world transition accompaniment — two musical registers: [old world musical theme] representing the [old world element] — established, slower, known — and [new world musical theme] representing the [new world element] — faster, brasher, arriving, the two themes present simultaneously in a contrapuntal texture, [transition event] given a brief musical resolution — the two themes finding a moment of harmony — before both continue on their separate trajectories, the world of 1920s transition in full acoustic argument with itself

1920s transitional world moment.default: horses and automobiles sharing the same street — the old world and the new
examples: the last horse-drawn tram and the first electric one on the same route
silent film cinema and the first talking picture theatre side by side
hand-set type printing press and the first mechanical typesetter in the same print shop

old world element.default: horse-drawn delivery wagons — the economy of centuries
examples: horse-drawn tram — the familiar route, the familiar technology
silent film cinema — the established art form
the compositor hand-setting type — the craftsman's art

new world element.default: early automobiles — the internal combustion engine as the future
examples: electric tram — faster, cleaner, louder, displacing
the talkie theatre — the new art form, the old one's replacement
the mechanical typesetter — faster, cheaper, requiring fewer hands

period population navigating change.default: delivery workers, pedestrians, policemen — managing the new traffic with rules built for the old
examples: commuters — choosing one or the other without quite knowing they are making a historical choice
cinema-goers — some going to the silent, some to the talkie, the two queues adjacent
print workers — some at the old press, some learning the new, the same room, different futures

transition architecture or object.default: a crossing point — where horse and automobile must negotiate passage
examples: the junction where both tram types pass — the transition visible as a track change
the block where both cinemas stand — their competing bills visible simultaneously
the print room floor — old press and new machine, the gap between them

old world activity.default: the horse-drawn deliveries continuing — reliable, slow, the city's circulatory system for a century
examples: the horse tram completing its route — the familiar stops, the familiar passengers
the silent film showing — the audience absorbed in the world without sound
the compositor setting type by hand — accuracy and craft in every letter

new world activity.default: automobiles navigating around the horse-drawn — faster, louder, impatient
examples: the electric tram overtaking — faster, and carrying twice as many
the talkie showing — laughter from inside, the new audience larger
the mechanical typesetter running — the speed making the craft beside it look slow

transition event.default: a horse startling at an automobile horn — the old world's biological response to the new world's technology
examples: a passenger choosing the electric tram over the horse tram — one small historical vote
a cinema-goer emerging from the talkie and pausing at the silent film's bill — the comparison made
a compositor watching the machine run — the calculation happening in real time

old world musical theme.default: steady walking bass — the horse's rhythm, the century's pace
examples: tram bell motif — regular, mechanical, familiar
silent film piano accompaniment style — the established vocabulary
compositor's rhythm — steady, hand-paced, craftsman's time

new world musical theme.default: syncopated upper register — jazz arriving, the automobile's rhythm
examples: electric hum in the bass — new frequency, new world
talkie brass and rhythm section — the sound arriving into the image
mechanical tempo — faster, regular, machine-paced

lens.default: 35mm equivalent wide — both worlds in the same frame
examples: 28mm equivalent
24mm equivalent — the full transition visible
50mm equivalent — the contrast more compressed

tool_notes: Sora handles the complex period transition world. The old and new sharing the same frame without drama — the transition happening in plain sight, unannounced — is the concept. Add '1920s world transition, silent film, orthochromatic, [old world element] and [new world element] simultaneously, historical change in progress, Charlie Chaplin era, 4:3'. The person who briefly sees the transition and then continues is the human register of the historical moment.
